proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: The plan hinges on securing government authorization, corporate sponsorship, and a custom smartphone app without detailing technical development, data validation, or logistics for managing 5 % city‑wide participation. These high‑level dependencies are not substantiated, making successful execution unlikely.

novelty.rationale: One‑day car‑free or walk‑to‑work events already exist worldwide; using an app to record steps is a common feature in many citizen‑science campaigns. The proposal adds few original mechanisms beyond standard awareness‑day formats.

impact.rationale: Emission savings are derived from a chain of assumptions (5 % participation, complete substitution of car trips, constant emission factors) that are not empirically validated. Potential rebound effects (e.g., increased car use on other days) are ignored, so the claimed 39 MtCO₂e yr⁻¹ global reduction is highly speculative.

presentation.rationale: The document is verbose, contains numerous typographical errors, and mixes narrative with unreferenced tables, making it difficult to extract a clear, testable methodology.

A1.perspective_summary: The proposal aims to raise climate awareness through a city‑wide walking day, tracked by a mobile app and incentivized with prizes. From a scientific standpoint, the causal link between a single event and long‑term emission reductions is weak, the required participation rates are optimistic, and the methodological details needed for rigorous impact assessment are missing.
key_concerns: Assumption that 5 % of Buenos Aires residents will voluntarily forgo all motorized commuting for one day.
No validated algorithm or calibration for converting steps/bike km into CO₂e savings; reliance on a generic footprint calculator.
Absence of a baseline measurement of current travel behavior to enable a counterfactual analysis.
Potential rebound effects (e.g., increased car use on other days) are not addressed.
Unclear funding and governance structure for app development, data privacy, and prize distribution.
Scaling extrapolations to national and global levels ignore demographic, infrastructural, and cultural differences.

feasibility.rationale: The plan depends on a single government authorization, undefined funding streams, and a private‑sector app that will collect location data. No clear legal basis for data privacy, no budget, and no designated lead agency are provided, making implementation highly uncertain.

novelty.rationale: Walk‑or‑bike‑to‑work days and citizen‑tracking apps already exist worldwide. The added element of linking the event to home‑office incentives and the local ecobici system is modestly original for Buenos Aires, but not a breakthrough.

impact.rationale: The estimated short‑term CO₂e savings (≈66 t / day) are negligible for a city of 2.9 M people, and the long‑term extrapolation (39 Mt / yr) rests on unsupported behavior‑change assumptions. Expected climate impact is therefore low.

presentation.rationale: The document is fragmented, contains numerous typographical errors, references figures that are not included, and mixes calculations with narrative without clear sourcing, hindering comprehension and credibility.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance standpoint, the proposal lacks a concrete institutional framework, reliable financing, and compliance with data‑privacy regulations. While the idea of a city‑wide walk day is socially appealing, the operational and legal scaffolding required to deliver the claimed emissions reductions is insufficiently articulated.
key_concerns: Absence of a designated lead agency (e.g., municipal transport or environment department) to coordinate the event.
No budget or cost‑recovery plan; reliance on undefined sponsorships (Google, media) raises conflict‑of‑interest risks.
Data‑privacy and security: the app will track individual movements without a clear legal basis or GDPR‑like safeguards.
Unrealistic behavior‑change assumptions (5 % participation, lasting habit formation) are not supported by evidence.
The proposal expects the government to mandate home‑office days without referencing labor‑law provisions or employer agreements.
Metrics and calculations are loosely sourced and lack sensitivity analysis, undermining credibility of impact claims.

feasibility.rationale: The event depends on simultaneous coordination of government permits, corporate home‑office policies, a custom smartphone app, prize funding, security, health services and media outreach. None of these elements are budgeted or secured, and the proposal offers no concrete implementation timeline or risk mitigation plan. Achieving 5 % city‑wide participation in a single day without strong, pre‑negotiated incentives is highly uncertain, making the operational risk substantial.

novelty.rationale: Walk‑or‑bike‑to‑work days and car‑free days are already common worldwide. Adding an app for step tracking and prize competitions is an incremental variation rather than a novel approach. The business‑model canvas and references to existing bike‑share programs further underline the lack of originality.

impact.rationale: The emissions‑saving calculations rely on optimistic assumptions (5 % participation, perfect substitution of car trips, linear GHG factors). Even if those numbers held, the short‑term saving of ~66 tCO₂e/day is negligible for a city of 2.9 M people, and the extrapolated 0.38 % of transport sector emissions globally is marginal. Behavioural change and long‑term habit formation are speculative and not substantiated with evidence.

presentation.rationale: The document is lengthy, contains numerous typographical errors, inconsistent tables, and vague references. Key operational details (budget, responsible parties, data‑privacy safeguards) are missing, making it difficult for reviewers to assess feasibility.

A3.perspective_summary: From an operational standpoint, LET'S WALK! is an ambitious awareness‑driven event that hinges on a complex web of stakeholder commitments, app development, and logistics that are not concretely planned or funded. While the concept aligns with existing mobility‑reduction campaigns, the proposal lacks clear implementation pathways, risk mitigation, and realistic adoption estimates, limiting its practical scalability.
key_concerns: Unclear funding sources for app development, prizes, security, and health services.
Reliance on government authorization and corporate agreement to allow home‑office days, which may be difficult to secure.
Optimistic participation target (5 % of city population) without a detailed incentive or outreach strategy.
Accuracy and validation of GHG‑savings calculations derived from self‑reported app data.
Data‑privacy and consent issues related to tracking individual movement and sharing it with sponsors.
Scalability to other cities/countries would require replicating the entire coordination framework each time.
Safety and liability risks associated with large numbers of pedestrians and cyclists on urban streets.

feasibility.rationale: Organising a city‑wide walk day with an app, prizes and security forces is doable, but the plan assumes 5 % participation, home‑office for many workers, and safe walking routes that do not exist in many barrios. The digital‑divide and lack of accessible sidewalks for people with disabilities make the target unrealistic for a large share of the population.

novelty.rationale: Walk‑or‑bike‑to‑work days and car‑free events already exist worldwide. Adding a tracking app and gamified prizes adds a modest twist, but the core idea is not new.

impact.rationale: The proposal quantifies a short‑term saving of ~66 tCO₂e/day (0.38 % of transport emissions) and long‑term savings that rely on behavioural change that is not guaranteed. Health benefits could be significant if inclusive, yet the plan overlooks people who cannot walk or bike, potentially widening inequities.

presentation.rationale: The document is lengthy, contains typographical errors, inconsistent tables, and calculations that are not fully sourced. The implementation timeline and responsibilities are vague, making it hard for stakeholders to assess.

A4.perspective_summary: From the community viewpoint, LET'S WALK! aims to raise climate awareness through a one‑day walking/biking challenge, but it risks excluding low‑income, disabled, and digitally‑unconnected residents. While the concept could foster healthier habits, the assumptions about participation, home‑office feasibility, and safe infrastructure are weak, limiting real‑world impact.
key_concerns: Lack of accessible sidewalks and bike lanes in many neighborhoods makes walking/biking unsafe for people with disabilities, the elderly, and low‑income residents.
The requirement to use a smartphone app excludes those without smartphones or reliable data plans, raising a digital‑divide issue.
Privacy of location and movement data collected by the app is not addressed; sponsors could misuse the big‑data.
Home‑office is unrealistic for informal or service‑sector workers who make up a large share of Buenos Aires' workforce.
Incentive prizes may primarily attract higher‑income participants, leaving vulnerable groups out of the benefits.
The 5 % participation target and downstream behavioural changes (recycling, solar adoption) are optimistic without concrete outreach and equity measures.

feasibility.rationale: Organising a city‑wide walking day with an app and prize incentives is technically possible, but the proposal glosses over major logistical hurdles: securing public funding for security and health staff, guaranteeing safe walking routes in low‑income neighborhoods, and obtaining consent for extensive location data. These equity‑related obstacles make the plan only moderately feasible.

novelty.rationale: Car‑free or walking‑day events already exist worldwide; the added element of a tracking app and corporate prizes is an incremental twist rather than a genuinely new approach to climate action.

impact.rationale: The estimated CO₂ savings (≈0.38 % of transport emissions) are modest and rely on optimistic participation (5 % of the population). Benefits accrue mainly to sponsors and the government, while the costs (security, health services, data infrastructure) are borne by the public. Moreover, the proposal does not address who is excluded (people with disabilities, long‑distance commuters, informal workers), limiting its distributive justice.

presentation.rationale: The document is lengthy, contains numerous typographical and grammatical errors, mixes tables, screenshots, and narrative without clear structure. Data sources are cited inconsistently, making it hard to verify claims.

A5.perspective_summary: From an ethics and justice standpoint, the "Let's Walk!" event raises serious distributional concerns: it privileges those who can already walk or bike short distances, while marginalising low‑income commuters, people with disabilities, and informal workers. The collection of detailed mobility data by private firms also creates privacy and exploitation risks, and the public sector bears most costs while private sponsors reap most benefits.
key_concerns: Exclusion of people with disabilities, long commutes, or unsafe neighborhoods – the proposal offers no mitigation measures.
Data privacy: the app shares granular location data with Google and corporate sponsors, potentially enabling surveillance and commercial exploitation.
Public cost vs private benefit: security, health personnel, and media spend are funded by the city, yet prizes and big‑data insights primarily reward sponsors.
Overstated emissions reductions: the calculations assume high participation and do not account for rebound effects (e.g., increased car use on other days).
Potential shift of burden onto remaining public transport users if large numbers avoid buses/subways for a single day.

feasibility.rationale: Organizing a city‑wide one‑day event, developing a tracking app, and securing government and corporate sponsorship are plausible, but the proposal lacks a concrete implementation plan, budget breakdown, and risk mitigation for low participation (5% of population) and security/health logistics.

novelty.rationale: The concept mirrors existing Car‑Free or Walk‑to‑Work days and the use of mobile apps for activity tracking; no fundamentally new technology, policy mechanism, or systemic intervention is introduced.

impact.rationale: Short‑term emission savings (~66 tCO₂e/day) are negligible relative to Buenos Aires’ total transport emissions, and the long‑term impact claims (recycling, solar law, electric buses) are speculative and not causally linked to a single event, making the overall climate impact weak.

presentation.rationale: The document is verbose, contains typographical errors, inconsistent units, and unclear methodology for key calculations, which hampers credibility and reproducibility.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long‑term sustainability standpoint, the proposal offers a modest awareness‑raising activity but overstates its systemic climate benefits. The reliance on a one‑day behavioral push without clear pathways to durable habit change limits its resilience and scalability.
key_concerns: Long‑term impact estimates (recycling increase, solar law adoption, electric bus expansion) are not substantiated by the event’s scope.
Assumes 5 % population participation without evidence; low uptake would collapse projected emission savings.
Potential equity issues: low‑income or mobility‑limited residents cannot easily replace car trips with walking or biking.
Risk of rebound effects if participants compensate later by increasing car use, negating short‑term gains.
Data accuracy and privacy concerns around the tracking app are not addressed.
No integration with broader urban transport planning or policy incentives beyond the event day.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on multiple high‑level dependencies (government authorization, corporate home‑office agreements, a custom app, security, health services, and sponsorship) that are not budgeted, legally scoped, or operationally detailed. The absence of a concrete implementation timeline, risk mitigation, and data‑privacy framework makes successful execution unlikely.

novelty.rationale: One‑day car‑free or walk‑to‑work events and citizen‑science tracking apps already exist worldwide. Adding a gamified prize system and linking to local bike‑share is only an incremental variation, not a breakthrough approach.

impact.rationale: Emission‑saving calculations depend on optimistic assumptions (5 % city‑wide participation, complete substitution of car trips, linear GHG factors) and ignore rebound effects. Even if those assumptions held, the short‑term saving (~66 tCO₂e/day) is negligible for a city of 2.9 M people, and the long‑term extrapolations are speculative and not causally linked to a single event.

presentation.rationale: The document is verbose, contains numerous typographical and formatting errors, references figures that are missing, and mixes narrative with calculations without clear sourcing, making it difficult to assess the proposal rigorously.

final.synthesis: The main points of contention were (1) feasibility – A4, A5 and A6 argued the event was technically doable, while A1‑A3 highlighted missing budgets, legal bases and data‑privacy safeguards. The weight of evidence (no budget, no lead agency, no privacy plan) led to a consensus that feasibility is low (score 2). (2) Novelty – A2 and A4 saw modest originality in tying home‑office incentives to the walk day; the majority viewed the concept as a standard awareness‑day variant, so a low novelty score (2) was chosen. (3) Impact – A4 gave a higher impact rating based on potential health benefits, but all other experts emphasized the speculative nature of the emissions estimates and the negligible climate effect, resulting in a low impact score (2). Presentation was uniformly judged weak. The final scores reflect the preponderance of critical assessments across the six expert perspectives.
final.uncertainty: Remaining uncertainty surrounds the magnitude of health and behavioural benefits that could arise if the event achieved high participation, and whether a more detailed implementation plan could substantially improve feasibility. The exact emissions reduction remains uncertain due to the reliance on unvalidated assumptions and lack of baseline data.
